**Tunisia**,[[b]](#cite_note-20) officially the **Republic of Tunisia**,[[c]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22) is a country in the [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb") region of [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"). It is bordered by [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria") to the west and southwest, [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya") to the southeast, and the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") to the north and east. Tunisia also shares maritime borders with [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") through the islands of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") and [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") to the north and [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") to the east. It features the archaeological sites of [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") dating back to the 9th century BC, as well as the Great Mosque of [Kairouan](/wiki/Kairouan "Kairouan"). Known for its ancient architecture, [souks](/wiki/Souks_of_Tunis "Souks of Tunis"), and blue coasts, it covers 163,610 km2 (63,170 sq mi), and has a population of 12.1 million. It contains the eastern end of the [Atlas Mountains](/wiki/Atlas_Mountains "Atlas Mountains") and the northern reaches of the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara") desert; much of its remaining territory is [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land"). Its 1,300 km (810 mi) of coastline includes the African conjunction of the western and eastern parts of the [Mediterranean Basin](/wiki/Mediterranean_Basin "Mediterranean Basin"). Tunisia is home to Africa's northernmost point, [Cape Angela](/wiki/Cape_Angela "Cape Angela"). Located on the northeastern coast, [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis") is the capital of the country, which is itself named after Tunis. The official language of Tunisia is [Arabic](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Arabic "Modern Standard Arabic"). The vast majority of Tunisia's population is [Arab](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") and [Muslim](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"). Vernacular [Tunisian Arabic](/wiki/Tunisian_Arabic "Tunisian Arabic") is the most spoken language, and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") serves as an administrative and educational language in some contexts, but has no official status.

Beginning in early antiquity, Tunisia was inhabited by the indigenous [Berbers](/wiki/Berbers "Berbers"). The [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia"), a [Semitic people](/wiki/Ancient_Semitic-speaking_peoples "Ancient Semitic-speaking peoples"), began to arrive in the 12th century BC, [settling on the coast and establishing several settlements](/wiki/Phoenician_settlement_of_North_Africa "Phoenician settlement of North Africa"), of which [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") emerged as the most powerful by the 7th century BC. The descendants of the Phoenician settlers came to be known as the [Punic people](/wiki/Punic_people "Punic people"). [Ancient Carthage](/wiki/Ancient_Carthage "Ancient Carthage") was a major [mercantile](/wiki/Trade "Trade") empire and a military rival to the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") until 146 BC when it was [defeated](/wiki/Siege_of_Carthage_(Third_Punic_War) "Siege of Carthage (Third Punic War)") by the Romans who occupied Tunisia for most of the next 800 years. The Romans introduced [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") and left architectural legacies like the [Amphitheatre of El Jem](/wiki/Amphitheatre_of_El_Jem "Amphitheatre of El Jem"). In the 7th century AD, [Arab Muslims conquered](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Maghreb "Muslim conquest of the Maghreb") Tunisia and [settled with their tribes and families](/wiki/Arab_migrations_to_the_Maghreb "Arab migrations to the Maghreb"), bringing [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and [Arab culture](/wiki/Arab_culture "Arab culture"). A later large-scale [Arab migration](/wiki/Arab_migrations_to_the_Maghreb#Banu_Hilal_and_Banu_Sulaym_(11th_century) "Arab migrations to the Maghreb") of [Banu Hilal](/wiki/Banu_Hilal "Banu Hilal") and [Banu Sulaym](/wiki/Banu_Sulaym "Banu Sulaym") tribes in the 11th-12th centuries accelerated this process. By around the 15th century, the region of modern-day Tunisia had already been almost completely [Arabized](/wiki/Arabization "Arabization").[[20]](#cite_note-23) Then, in 1546, the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") established control, holding sway until 1881, when the [French conquered Tunisia](/wiki/French_conquest_of_Tunisia "French conquest of Tunisia"). In 1956, [Tunisia gained independence](/wiki/Tunisian_independence "Tunisian independence") as the Tunisian Republic. Today, [Tunisia's culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Tunisia "Culture of Tunisia") and identity are rooted in this centuries-long intersection of different cultures and ethnicities.

In 2011, the [Tunisian Revolution](/wiki/Tunisian_Revolution "Tunisian Revolution"), which was triggered by dissatisfaction with the lack of freedom and [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") under the 24-year rule of President [Zine El Abidine Ben Ali](/wiki/Zine_El_Abidine_Ben_Ali "Zine El Abidine Ben Ali"), overthrew his regime and catalyzed the broader [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring") movement across the region. Free multiparty parliamentary [elections](/wiki/2011_Tunisian_Constituent_Assembly_election "2011 Tunisian Constituent Assembly election") were held shortly thereafter; the country again voted for parliament on 26 October 2014,[[21]](#cite_note-24) and for president on 23 November 2014.[[22]](#cite_note-25) From 2014 to 2020, it was considered the only democratic state in the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world"), according to *[The Economist Democracy Index](/wiki/The_Economist_Democracy_Index "The Economist Democracy Index")*.[[23]](#cite_note-26)[[d]](#cite_note-27) After [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding") under President [Kais Saied](/wiki/Kais_Saied "Kais Saied"), Tunisia was rated a [hybrid regime](/wiki/Hybrid_regime "Hybrid regime") in 2022[[24]](#cite_note-28) and as of 2025 it no longer has free and fair elections according to [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House").[[25]](#cite_note-29) It is one of the few countries in Africa ranking high on the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"), with one of the [highest per capita incomes](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of African countries by GDP (PPP) per capita") on the continent, ranking 129th in [GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita") income.

Tunisia is well integrated into the international community. It is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), *[Organisation internationale de la Francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation internationale de la Francophonie")*, the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League"), the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation"), the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union"), the [Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa](/wiki/Common_Market_for_Eastern_and_Southern_Africa "Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa"), the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"), the [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court"), the [Group of 77](/wiki/Group_of_77 "Group of 77"), among others. It maintains close economic and political relations with some European countries, particularly with [France](/wiki/France%E2%80%93Tunisia_relations "France–Tunisia relations") and [Italy](/wiki/Italy%E2%80%93Tunisia_relations "Italy–Tunisia relations"),[[26]](#cite_note-30)[[27]](#cite_note-31) due to their geographical proximity. Tunisia also has an [association agreement](/wiki/European_Union_Association_Agreement "European Union Association Agreement") with the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and has attained the status of a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

## Etymology

See also: [Tunis § Etymology](/wiki/Tunis#Etymology "Tunis")

The word *Tunisia* is derived from [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis"), a central urban hub and the capital of modern-day Tunisia. The present form of the name, with its [Latinate](/wiki/Latin "Latin") suffix *-ia*, evolved from [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") *Tunisie*,[[28]](#cite_note-Adrian-32)[[29]](#cite_note-33) in turn generally associated with the [Berber](/wiki/Berber_languages "Berber languages") root ⵜⵏⵙ, transcribed *tns*, which means "to lay down" or "encampment".[[30]](#cite_note-peter-ross-34) It is sometimes also associated with the [Carthage goddess](/wiki/Carthaginian_religion "Carthaginian religion") [Tanith](/wiki/Tanit "Tanit") (or Tunit),[[28]](#cite_note-Adrian-32)[[31]](#cite_note-35) and the ancient city of Tynes.[[32]](#cite_note-ej-brill-36)[[33]](#cite_note-hann-war-37)

The French derivative *Tunisie* was adopted in some European languages with slight modifications, introducing a distinctive name to designate the country. Other languages have left the name untouched, such as the [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") Туни́с (*Tunís*) and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") *Túnez*. In this case, the same name is used for both country and city, as with the [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") تونس, and only by context can one tell the difference.[[28]](#cite_note-Adrian-32)

In English, Tunisia before independence was also often called simply "Tunis", a name that persisted until the 1940s;[[e]](#cite_note-43) under French influence, the neologism "Tunisia", adapted from *Tunisie*, gradually took hold.[[28]](#cite_note-Adrian-32) The adjective "Tunisian" first appeared in English in 1825;[[39]](#cite_note-44) the previous adjectival form was "Tunisine".[[40]](#cite_note-45)

## History

Main article: [History of Tunisia](/wiki/History_of_Tunisia "History of Tunisia")

### Antiquity

Main articles: [Capsian culture](/wiki/Capsian_culture "Capsian culture") and [Ancient Carthage](/wiki/Ancient_Carthage "Ancient Carthage")

Farming methods reached the [Nile Valley](/wiki/Nile "Nile") from the [Fertile Crescent](/wiki/Fertile_Crescent "Fertile Crescent") region about 5000 BC, and spread to the [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb") by about 4000 BC. Agricultural communities in the humid coastal plains of central Tunisia then were ancestors of today's [Berber](/wiki/Berber_people "Berber people") tribes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Carthaginianempire.PNG/330px-Carthaginianempire.PNG)](/wiki/File:Carthaginianempire.PNG)

[Carthaginian](/wiki/Punic_people "Punic people") dependencies and protectorates through the [Punic Wars](/wiki/Punic_Wars "Punic Wars")

It was believed in ancient times that Africa was originally populated by [Gaetulians](/wiki/Gaetuli "Gaetuli") and Libyans, both nomadic peoples. According to the Roman historian [Sallust](/wiki/Sallust "Sallust"), the demigod Hercules died in Spain and his polyglot eastern army was left to settle the land, with some [migrating](/wiki/Human_migration "Human migration") to Africa. Persians went to the West and intermarried with the Gaetulians and became the Numidians. The Medes settled and were known as Mauri, later Moors.[[41]](#cite_note-46)

The Numidians and Moors belonged to the race from which the Berbers are descended. The translated meaning of Numidian is Nomad and indeed the people were semi-nomadic until the reign of [Masinissa](/wiki/Masinissa "Masinissa") of the Massyli tribe.[[42]](#cite_note-47)[[43]](#cite_note-48)[[44]](#cite_note-49)

At the beginning of recorded history, Tunisia was inhabited by [Berber](/wiki/Berber_people "Berber people") tribes. Its coast was settled by [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") starting as early as the 12th century BC ([Bizerte](/wiki/Bizerte "Bizerte"), [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_Tunisia "Utica, Tunisia")). The city of [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") was founded in the 9th century BC by Phoenicians. Legend says that [Dido](/wiki/Dido_(Queen_of_Carthage) "Dido (Queen of Carthage)") from Tyre, now in modern-day Lebanon, founded the city in 814 BC, as retold by the [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") writer [Timaeus of Tauromenium](/wiki/Timaeus_(historian) "Timaeus (historian)"). The settlers of Carthage brought their culture and religion from Phoenicia, now present-day [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") and adjacent areas.[[45]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Mommsen_p265.jpg/250px-Mommsen_p265.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mommsen_p265.jpg)

Statue of the Carthaginian general [Hannibal Barca](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal")

After the series of wars with Greek city-states of Sicily in the 5th century BC, Carthage rose to power and eventually became the dominant civilization in the Western [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean"). The people of Carthage worshipped a pantheon of Middle Eastern gods including [Baal](/wiki/Baal "Baal") and [Tanit](/wiki/Tanit "Tanit"). Tanit's symbol, a simple female figure with extended arms and long dress, is a popular icon found in ancient sites. The founders of Carthage also established a [Tophet](/wiki/Tophet "Tophet"), which was altered in Roman times.

A Carthaginian invasion of Italy led by [Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal") during the [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War"), one of a series of wars with [Rome](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), nearly crippled the rise of Roman power. From the conclusion of the Second Punic War in 202 BC, Carthage functioned as a client state of the Roman Republic for another 50 years.[[46]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Temple_du_Capitole_%28Dougga%29.JPG/250px-Temple_du_Capitole_%28Dougga%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Temple_du_Capitole_(Dougga).JPG)

Ruins of a 2nd-century temple in [Dougga](/wiki/Dougga "Dougga"), one of nine [World Heritage Sites in Tunisia](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Tunisia "List of World Heritage Sites in Tunisia")

Following the [Battle of Carthage](/wiki/Battle_of_Carthage_(c._149_BC) "Battle of Carthage (c. 149 BC)") which began in 149 BC during the [Third Punic War](/wiki/Third_Punic_War "Third Punic War"), Carthage was conquered by Rome in 146 BC.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteel20139–10-52) Following its conquest, the Romans renamed Carthage to [Africa](/wiki/Africa_(Roman_province) "Africa (Roman province)"), incorporating it as a province.

Berber bishop [Donatus Magnus](/wiki/Donatus_Magnus "Donatus Magnus") was the founder of a Christian group known as the [Donatists](/wiki/Donatist "Donatist").[[48]](#cite_note-53) During the 5th and 6th centuries (from 430 to 533 AD), the Germanic [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals") invaded and ruled over a kingdom in Northwest Africa that included present-day Tripoli. The region was easily reconquered in 533–534 AD, during the rule of Emperor [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I"), by the [Eastern Romans](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") led by General [Belisarius](/wiki/Belisarius "Belisarius"),[[49]](#cite_note-54) preluding a 165-year era of [Byzantine rule](/wiki/Byzantine_North_Africa "Byzantine North Africa").

### Middle Ages

Main article: [History of medieval Tunisia](/wiki/History_of_medieval_Tunisia "History of medieval Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Statue_de_Okba_ibn_Nafi_al_Fihri_en_Alg%C3%A9rie.jpg/250px-Statue_de_Okba_ibn_Nafi_al_Fihri_en_Alg%C3%A9rie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_de_Okba_ibn_Nafi_al_Fihri_en_Alg%C3%A9rie.jpg)

[Uqba ibn Nafi](/wiki/Uqba_ibn_Nafi "Uqba ibn Nafi") led the [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad "Umayyad") conquest of Tunisia in the late 7th century.

Sometime between the second half of the 7th century and the early part of the 8th century, [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") [Muslim conquest occurred in the region](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Maghreb "Muslim conquest of the Maghreb"). They founded the first Islamic city in Northwest Africa, [Kairouan](/wiki/Kairouan "Kairouan"). It was there in 670 AD that the [Mosque of Uqba](/wiki/Mosque_of_Uqba "Mosque of Uqba"), or the Great Mosque of Kairouan, was constructed.[[50]](#cite_note-55) This mosque is the oldest and most prestigious sanctuary in the Muslim West with the oldest standing [minaret](/wiki/Minaret "Minaret") in the world;[[51]](#cite_note-56) it is also considered a masterpiece of Islamic art and architecture.[[52]](#cite_note-57) The [Arab migration to the Maghreb](/wiki/Arab_migration_to_the_Maghreb "Arab migration to the Maghreb") began during this time.

The region in its entirety was taken in 695, retaken by the Byzantine Eastern Romans in 697, but lost permanently in 698. The transition from a Latin-speaking Christian Berber society to a Muslim and mostly Arabic-speaking society took over 400 years (the equivalent process in Egypt and the Fertile Crescent took 600 years) and resulted in the final disappearance of Christianity and Latin in the 12th or 13th century. The majority of the population were not Muslim until quite late in the 9th century; a vast majority were during the 10th. Also, some Tunisian Christians emigrated; some richer members of society did so after the conquest in 698 and others were welcomed by Norman rulers to Sicily or Italy in the 11th and 12th centuries – the logical destination because of the 1200 year close connection between the two regions.[[53]](#cite_note-58)

The Arab governors of Tunis founded the [Aghlabid dynasty](/wiki/Aghlabids "Aghlabids"), which ruled Tunisia, [Tripolitania](/wiki/Tripolitania "Tripolitania") and eastern Algeria from 800 to 909.[[54]](#cite_note-lapidus-59) Tunisia flourished under Arab rule when extensive systems were constructed to supply towns with water for household use and irrigation that promoted agriculture (especially olive production).[[54]](#cite_note-lapidus-59)[[55]](#cite_note-tunisia-lp-60) This prosperity permitted luxurious court life and was marked by the construction of new palace cities such as al-Abbasiya (809) and Raq Adda (877).[[54]](#cite_note-lapidus-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Panorama_of_the_courtyard_of_the_Great_Mosque_of_Kairouan.jpg/330px-Panorama_of_the_courtyard_of_the_Great_Mosque_of_Kairouan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panorama_of_the_courtyard_of_the_Great_Mosque_of_Kairouan.jpg)

Domes of the [Great Mosque of Kairouan](/wiki/Mosque_of_Uqba "Mosque of Uqba"). Founded in 670, it dates in its present form largely from the Aghlabid period (9th century). It is the oldest mosque in the [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb").

After conquering [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), the [Fatimids](/wiki/Fatimids "Fatimids") abandoned Tunisia and parts of Eastern Algeria to the local [Zirids](/wiki/Zirids "Zirids") (972–1148).[[56]](#cite_note-stearns-61) Zirid Tunisia flourished in many areas: agriculture, industry, trade, and religious and secular learning.[[57]](#cite_note-locfatamids-62) Management by the later Zirid [emirs](/wiki/Emirs "Emirs") was neglectful though, and political instability was connected to the decline of Tunisian trade and agriculture.[[54]](#cite_note-lapidus-59)[[58]](#cite_note-islamic-dynasties-63)[[59]](#cite_note-history-of-africa-64)

The depredation of the Tunisian campaigns by the [Banu Hilal](/wiki/Banu_Hilal "Banu Hilal"), a warlike Arab tribe encouraged by the Fatimids of Egypt to seize Northwest Africa, sent the region's rural and urban economic life into further decline.[[56]](#cite_note-stearns-61) Consequently, the region underwent rapid urbanisation as famines depopulated the countryside and industry shifted from agriculture to manufactures.[[60]](#cite_note-65) The Arab historian [Ibn Khaldun](/wiki/Ibn_Khaldun "Ibn Khaldun") wrote that the lands ravaged by Banu Hilal invaders had become completely arid desert.[[58]](#cite_note-islamic-dynasties-63)[[61]](#cite_note-66)

The main Tunisian cities were conquered by the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") under the [Kingdom of Africa](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Africa "Kingdom of Africa") in the 12th century, but following [the conquest of Tunisia](/wiki/Almohad_conquest_of_Ifriqiya "Almohad conquest of Ifriqiya") in 1159–1160 by the [Almohads](/wiki/Almohads "Almohads") the Normans were evacuated to Sicily. Communities of Tunisian Christians would still exist in [Nefzaoua](/wiki/Nefzaoua "Nefzaoua") up to the 14th century.[[62]](#cite_note-hrbek-67) The Almohads initially ruled over Tunisia through a governor, usually a near relative of the Caliph. Despite the prestige of the new masters, the country was still unruly, with continuous rioting and fighting between the townsfolk and wandering Arabs and Turks, the latter being subjects of the Muslim Armenian adventurer Karakush. Also, Tunisia was occupied by [Ayyubids](/wiki/Ayyubids "Ayyubids") between 1182 and 1183 and again between 1184 and 1187.[[63]](#cite_note-68)

The greatest threat to Almohad rule in Tunisia was the [Banu Ghaniya](/wiki/Banu_Ghaniya "Banu Ghaniya"), relatives of the [Almoravids](/wiki/Almoravids "Almoravids"), who from their base in [Mallorca](/wiki/Mallorca "Mallorca") tried to restore Almoravid rule over the Maghreb. Around 1200 they succeeded in extending their rule over the whole of Tunisia until they were crushed by Almohad troops in 1207. After this success, the Almohads installed Walid Abu Hafs as the governor of Tunisia. Tunisia remained part of the Almohad state, until 1230 when the son of Abu Hafs declared himself independent.

During the reign of the [Hafsid dynasty](/wiki/Hafsid_dynasty "Hafsid dynasty") from their capital Tunis, fruitful commercial relationships were established with several Christian Mediterranean states.[[64]](#cite_note-69) In the late 16th century the coast became a [pirate](/wiki/Pirate "Pirate") stronghold.

### Ottoman Tunisia

Main article: [Ottoman Tunisia](/wiki/Ottoman_Tunisia "Ottoman Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Frans_Hogenberg_battle_of_Tunis.jpg/250px-Frans_Hogenberg_battle_of_Tunis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frans_Hogenberg_battle_of_Tunis.jpg)

[Conquest of Tunis](/wiki/Conquest_of_Tunis_(1535) "Conquest of Tunis (1535)") by [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") and liberation of Christian [galley slaves](/wiki/Galley_slave "Galley slave") in 1535

In the last years of the [Hafsid dynasty](/wiki/Hafsid_dynasty "Hafsid dynasty"), Spain seized many of the coastal cities, but these were recovered by the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire").

The [first Ottoman conquest of Tunis](/wiki/Conquest_of_Tunis_(1534) "Conquest of Tunis (1534)") took place in 1534 under the command of [Barbarossa Hayreddin Pasha](/wiki/Hayreddin_Barbarossa "Hayreddin Barbarossa"), the younger brother of Oruç Reis, who was the [Kapudan Pasha](/wiki/Kapudan_Pasha "Kapudan Pasha") of the [Ottoman Fleet](/wiki/Ottoman_Navy "Ottoman Navy") during the reign of [Suleiman the Magnificent](/wiki/Suleiman_the_Magnificent "Suleiman the Magnificent"). However, it was not until the [final Ottoman reconquest of Tunis from Spain](/wiki/Conquest_of_Tunis_(1574) "Conquest of Tunis (1574)") in 1574 under Kapudan Pasha [Uluç Ali Reis](/wiki/Ulu%C3%A7_Ali_Reis "Uluç Ali Reis") that the Ottomans permanently acquired the former [Hafsid Tunisia](/wiki/History_of_medieval_Tunisia#Hafsid_dynasty_of_Tunis "History of medieval Tunisia"), retaining it until the [French conquest of Tunisia](/wiki/French_conquest_of_Tunisia "French conquest of Tunisia") in 1881.

Initially under Turkish rule from Algiers, soon the [Ottoman Porte](/wiki/Ottoman_Porte "Ottoman Porte") appointed directly for [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis") a governor called the [Pasha](/wiki/Pasha "Pasha") supported by [janissary](/wiki/Janissary "Janissary") forces. Before long, however, Tunisia became in effect an autonomous province, under the local [bey](/wiki/Bey "Bey"). Under its [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_People "Turkish People") governors, the beys, Tunisia attained virtual independence. The [Hussein dynasty](/wiki/Husainid_Dynasty "Husainid Dynasty") of beys, established in 1705, lasted until 1957.[[65]](#cite_note-70) This evolution of status was from time to time challenged without success by Algiers. During this era, the governing councils controlling Tunisia remained largely composed of a foreign elite who continued to conduct state business in the [Turkish language](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/St_Louis_Cathedral_-_Carthage_-_Tunisia_-_1899.jpg/250px-St_Louis_Cathedral_-_Carthage_-_Tunisia_-_1899.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Louis_Cathedral_-_Carthage_-_Tunisia_-_1899.jpg)

St Louis Cathedral – Carthage – Tunisia – 1899

Attacks on European shipping were made by [corsairs](/wiki/Barbary_corsairs "Barbary corsairs"), primarily from Algiers, but also from Tunis and [Tripoli](/wiki/Tripoli,_Libya "Tripoli, Libya"), yet after a long period of declining raids the growing power of the European states finally forced its termination.

The [plague epidemics](/wiki/Second_plague_pandemic "Second plague pandemic") ravaged Tunisia in 1784–1785, 1796–1797 and 1818–1820.[[66]](#cite_note-71)

In the 19th century, the rulers of Tunisia became aware of the ongoing efforts at political and social [reform in the Ottoman capital](/wiki/Tanzimat "Tanzimat"). The Bey of Tunis then, by his own lights but informed by the Turkish example, attempted to effect a modernizing reform of institutions and the economy.[[67]](#cite_note-Clancy-Smith1997-72) Tunisian international debt grew unmanageable. This was the reason or pretext for French forces to establish a [protectorate](/wiki/History_of_French_era_Tunisia "History of French era Tunisia") in 1881.[[68]](#cite_note-73)[[69]](#cite_note-74)

### French Protectorate of Tunisia (1881–1956)

Main article: [French protectorate of Tunisia](/wiki/French_protectorate_of_Tunisia "French protectorate of Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/A_Churchill_tank_and_other_vehicles_parade_through_Tunis%2C_8_May_1943._NA2880.jpg/250px-A_Churchill_tank_and_other_vehicles_parade_through_Tunis%2C_8_May_1943._NA2880.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Churchill_tank_and_other_vehicles_parade_through_Tunis,_8_May_1943._NA2880.jpg)

British tank moves through Tunis after the city was taken from Axis troops, 8 May 1943.

In 1869, Tunisia declared itself bankrupt and an international financial commission took control over its economy. In 1881, using the pretext of a Tunisian incursion into [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"), the French invaded with an army of about 36,000 and forced the Bey of Tunis, [Muhammad III as-Sadiq](/wiki/Muhammad_III_as-Sadiq "Muhammad III as-Sadiq"), to agree to the terms of the 1881 [Treaty of Bardo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bardo "Treaty of Bardo").[[70]](#cite_note-75) With this treaty, Tunisia was officially made a [French protectorate](/wiki/French_occupation_of_Tunisia "French occupation of Tunisia"), over the objections of Italy. European settlements in the country were actively encouraged; the number of [French](/wiki/French_people "French people") colonists grew from 34,000 in 1906 to 144,000 in 1945. In 1910 there were also 105,000 [Italians in Tunisia](/wiki/Italian_Tunisians "Italian Tunisians").[[71]](#cite_note-76)

During the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the protectorate of Tunisia was controlled by the collaborationist [Vichy](/wiki/Vichy_France "Vichy France") government in Metropolitan France. The antisemitic [Statute on Jews](/wiki/Vichy_anti-Jewish_legislation "Vichy anti-Jewish legislation") enacted by the Vichy government was also implemented in Vichy-controlled Northwest Africa and other overseas French territories. Thus, the persecution and murder of the Jews from 1940 to 1943 was part of [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") in France.

From November 1942 until May 1943, Vichy-controlled Tunisia was occupied by Germany. [SS](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel") Commander [Walter Rauff](/wiki/Walter_Rauff "Walter Rauff") continued to implement the "Final Solution" there. From 1942 to 1943, Tunisia was the scene of the [Tunisia Campaign](/wiki/Tunisia_Campaign "Tunisia Campaign"), a series of battles between the [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") and [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") forces. The battle opened with initial success by the German and Italian forces, but the massive supply and numerical superiority of the Allies led to the [Axis surrender](/wiki/Tunisian_Campaign "Tunisian Campaign") on 13 May 1943.[[72]](#cite_note-77)[[73]](#cite_note-78) The six-month campaign of Tunisia's liberation from Axis occupation signalled the end of the war in Africa.

### Struggle for independence (1943–1956)

After the liberation of Tunisia from the Germans, the French regained control over the government and made participation in a nationalist party illegal once more.[[74]](#cite_note-:02-79) [Moncef Bey](/wiki/Moncef_Bey "Moncef Bey"), who was popular amongst Tunisians, was deposed by the French.[[74]](#cite_note-:02-79) The French claimed that his removal was due to him being sympathetic to the Axis countries during German occupation, but the real reason is up for debate.[[74]](#cite_note-:02-79)

In 1945 after escaping French surveillance, Tunisian nationalist [Habib Bourguiba](/wiki/Habib_Bourguiba "Habib Bourguiba") arrived in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo").[[74]](#cite_note-:02-79) While there, he was able to make contact with the [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League").[[74]](#cite_note-:02-79) Later in 1946, after traveling to other Middle Eastern countries, he made his way to the United States to speak to both the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") at their headquarters at [Lake Success](/wiki/Lake_Success,_New_York "Lake Success, New York") and [U.S. State Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") officials in [Washington D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), pleading the case of the Tunisian nationalists.[[74]](#cite_note-:02-79)

As part of postwar Tunisia, a new all-Tunisian labor organization was formed, the [Union Générale des Travailleurs (UGTT)](/wiki/Tunisian_General_Labour_Union "Tunisian General Labour Union").[[74]](#cite_note-:02-79) This was one of the stronger components of the nationalist group [Neo-Destour](/wiki/Neo-Destour "Neo-Destour").[[74]](#cite_note-:02-79)

Habib Bourguiba made his way to the United States on 13 September 1949.[[75]](#cite_note-:12-80) He attended the [American Federation of Labor](/wiki/American_Federation_of_Labor "American Federation of Labor") meeting in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), California.[[75]](#cite_note-:12-80) The French were opposed to his presence there, and the US feared political change in North Africa due to the looming presence of possible [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") communist expansion.[[75]](#cite_note-:12-80)

Bourguiba continued to plead to foreign leaders when he traveled to Italy on 6 November 1951.[[75]](#cite_note-:12-80) His contacts included [Alberto Mellini Ponce De León](/w/index.php?title=Alberto_Mellini_Ponce_De_Le%C3%B3n&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alberto Mellini Ponce De León (page does not exist)"), [Mario Toscano](/w/index.php?title=Mario_Toscano&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mario Toscano (page does not exist)"), and [Licinio Vestri](/w/index.php?title=Licinio_Vestri&action=edit&redlink=1 "Licinio Vestri (page does not exist)").[[75]](#cite_note-:12-80) De León was an old friend of Bourguiba who had helped free him from German captivity, Toscano was the head of the *Ufficio Studi e Documentazione* in the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Italy) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Italy)"), and Vestri was an [Africanist](/wiki/Africanist_(discipline) "Africanist (discipline)") scholar.[[75]](#cite_note-:12-80) Despite his best efforts, the Italians remained neutral as they did not want to ruin relations with [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") ally France, nor did they want to hamper any possibility of future relations with Tunisia as it served as a key part of the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea").[[75]](#cite_note-:12-80)

The French Resident General in Tunisia, [Jean de Hautecloque](/w/index.php?title=Jean_de_Hautecloque&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jean de Hautecloque (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean_de_Hauteclocque "de:Jean de Hauteclocque"); [fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean_de_Hautecloque "fr:Jean de Hautecloque")] left Tunis to go to Paris on 25 August 1953, when he was replaced by [Pierre Voizard](/wiki/Pierre_Voizard "Pierre Voizard").[[76]](#cite_note-:2-81) Voizard had previously been the French Minister to [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco").[[76]](#cite_note-:2-81) A month after his arrival in Tunis on 26 September 1953, Voizard made many changes to ease tensions in Tunisia.[[76]](#cite_note-:2-81) He lifted press censorship and freed several political prisoners.[[76]](#cite_note-:2-81) He also restored the full powers of civil authorities and raised the state of siege in the [Sahel](/wiki/Sahel,_Tunisia "Sahel, Tunisia").[[76]](#cite_note-:2-81)

On 26 January 1954, Voizard announced that there would soon be new reforms in favor of granting more sovereignty to Tunisians while insuring the interests of the French and French citizens in Tunisia, at the Cercle Republicain d'outre Mer in Paris.[[76]](#cite_note-:2-81) The Neo-Destour group was not in favor of these reforms if they themselves were not involved in their creation.[[76]](#cite_note-:2-81) They also demanded the freedom of Bourguiba who was imprisoned on the Isle of Galete.[[76]](#cite_note-:2-81)

### Post-independence (1956–2011)

Main article: [History of modern Tunisia](/wiki/History_of_modern_Tunisia "History of modern Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Habib_Bourguiba_portrait4.jpg/250px-Habib_Bourguiba_portrait4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Habib_Bourguiba_portrait4.jpg)

[Habib Bourguiba](/wiki/Habib_Bourguiba "Habib Bourguiba") was the first president of Tunisia, from 1957 to 1987.

Tunisia achieved independence from France on 20 March 1956 with [Habib Bourguiba](/wiki/Habib_Bourguiba "Habib Bourguiba") as Prime Minister.[[77]](#cite_note-82) 20 March is celebrated annually as Tunisian Independence Day.[[78]](#cite_note-83) A year later, Tunisia was declared a republic, with Bourguiba as [the first President](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_Tunisia "List of Presidents of Tunisia").[[79]](#cite_note-BBC-84) From independence in 1956 until the 2011 revolution, the government and the [Constitutional Democratic Rally](/wiki/Constitutional_Democratic_Rally "Constitutional Democratic Rally") (RCD), formerly [Neo Destour](/wiki/Neo_Destour "Neo Destour") and the [Socialist Destourian Party](/wiki/Socialist_Destourian_Party "Socialist Destourian Party"), were effectively one. Following a report by [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"), *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* called Tunisia "one of the most modern but repressive countries in the Arab world".[[80]](#cite_note-85) On 12 May 1964, Tunisia nationalized foreign farmlands.[[81]](#cite_note-:1-86) Immediately after, France canceled all financial assistance for the country, which was to amount to more than $40 million.[[81]](#cite_note-:1-86) This led to the Tunisian National Assembly passing a bill that required all residents of the country to subscribe to a "popular loan" in proportion to their income.[[81]](#cite_note-:1-86) From 1977 until 2005, Tunisia was a shooting location for five films of the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars_(franchise) "Star Wars (franchise)")* film franchise.

In 1982, Tunisia became the center of the [Palestine Liberation Organization](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organization "Palestine Liberation Organization"), based in the capital Tunis. On 1 October 1985, the [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") [bombed the PLO Headquarters](/wiki/Operation_Wooden_Leg "Operation Wooden Leg"), killing at least 60 people.[[82]](#cite_note-:22-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Zine_El_Abidine_Ben_Ali.jpg/250px-Zine_El_Abidine_Ben_Ali.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zine_El_Abidine_Ben_Ali.jpg)

[Zine El Abidine Ben Ali](/wiki/Zine_El_Abidine_Ben_Ali "Zine El Abidine Ben Ali"), president of Tunisia from 1987 to 2011

In November 1987, doctors declared Bourguiba unfit to rule[[83]](#cite_note-88) and, in a bloodless coup d'état, Prime Minister [Zine El Abidine Ben Ali](/wiki/Zine_El_Abidine_Ben_Ali "Zine El Abidine Ben Ali") assumed the presidency[[79]](#cite_note-BBC-84) in accordance with Article 57 of the [Tunisian constitution](/wiki/Tunisian_constitution "Tunisian constitution").[[84]](#cite_note-89) The anniversary of Ben Ali's succession, 7 November, was celebrated as a national holiday. He was consistently re-elected with enormous majorities every five years (well over 80 percent of the vote), the last being 25 October 2009,[[85]](#cite_note-90) until he fled the country amid popular unrest in January 2011.

Ben Ali and his family were accused of corruption[[86]](#cite_note-sandiegounion-tribune.com-91) and plundering the country's money. Economic liberalisation provided further opportunities for financial mismanagement,[[87]](#cite_note-92) while corrupt members of the Trabelsi family, most notably in the cases of [Imed Trabelsi](/wiki/Imed_Trabelsi "Imed Trabelsi") and [Belhassen Trabelsi](/wiki/Belhassen_Trabelsi "Belhassen Trabelsi"), controlled much of the business sector in the country.[[88]](#cite_note-93) The First Lady [Leila Ben Ali](/wiki/Leila_Ben_Ali "Leila Ben Ali") was described as an "unabashed [shopaholic](/wiki/Oniomania "Oniomania")" who used the state airplane to make frequent unofficial trips to Europe's fashion capitals.[[89]](#cite_note-94) Tunisia refused a French request for the extradition of two of the President's nephews, from Leila's side, who were accused by the French State prosecutor of having stolen two mega-yachts from a French marina.[[90]](#cite_note-95) According to *[Le Monde](/wiki/Le_Monde "Le Monde")*, Ben Ali's son-in-law was being primed to eventually take over the country.[[91]](#cite_note-96)

Independent human rights groups, such as [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"), [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House"), and Protection International, documented that basic human and political rights were not respected.[[92]](#cite_note-97)[[93]](#cite_note-98) The regime obstructed in any way possible the work of local human rights organizations.[[94]](#cite_note-99) In 2008, in terms of [press freedom](/wiki/Press_freedom "Press freedom"), Tunisia was ranked 143rd out of 173.[[95]](#cite_note-100)

### Post-revolution (since 2011)

See also: [Tunisian Revolution](/wiki/Tunisian_Revolution "Tunisian Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Tunisia_Unrest_-_VOA_-_Tunis_14_Jan_2011_%282%29.jpg/250px-Tunisia_Unrest_-_VOA_-_Tunis_14_Jan_2011_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tunisia_Unrest_-_VOA_-_Tunis_14_Jan_2011_(2).jpg)

Tunis on 14 January 2011 during the [Tunisian Revolution](/wiki/Tunisian_Revolution "Tunisian Revolution")

The Tunisian Revolution[[96]](#cite_note-101)[[97]](#cite_note-102) was an intensive campaign of [civil resistance](/wiki/Civil_resistance "Civil resistance") that was precipitated by high [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment"), [food inflation](/wiki/Food_inflation "Food inflation"), [corruption](/wiki/Corruption "Corruption"),[[98]](#cite_note-103) a lack of [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") and other [political freedoms](/wiki/Political_freedom "Political freedom")[[99]](#cite_note-104) and poor [living conditions](/wiki/Living_conditions "Living conditions"). Labour unions were said to be an integral part of the protests.[[100]](#cite_note-105) The protests inspired the [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring"), a wave of similar actions throughout the Arab world. The catalyst for mass demonstrations was the death of [Mohamed Bouazizi](/wiki/Mohamed_Bouazizi "Mohamed Bouazizi"), a 26-year-old Tunisian street vendor, who set himself afire on 17 December 2010 in protest at the confiscation of his wares and the humiliation inflicted on him by a municipal official named [Faida Hamdy](/wiki/Faida_Hamdy "Faida Hamdy"). Anger and violence intensified following Bouazizi's death on 4 January 2011, ultimately leading longtime [President](/wiki/President_of_Tunisia "President of Tunisia") [Zine El Abidine Ben Ali](/wiki/Zine_El_Abidine_Ben_Ali "Zine El Abidine Ben Ali") to resign and flee the country on 14 January 2011, after 23 years in power.[[101]](#cite_note-106)

Protests continued for banning of the ruling party and the eviction of all its members from the transitional government formed by [Mohammed Ghannouchi](/wiki/Mohamed_Ghannouchi "Mohamed Ghannouchi"). Eventually the new government gave in to the demands. A Tunis court banned the ex-ruling party RCD and confiscated all its resources. A decree by the minister of the interior banned the "political police", special forces which were used to intimidate and persecute political activists.[[102]](#cite_note-107)

On 3 March 2011, the interim president announced that [elections to a Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Tunisian_Constituent_Assembly_election,_2011 "Tunisian Constituent Assembly election, 2011") would be held on 24 July 2011.[[103]](#cite_note-108) On 9 June 2011, the prime minister announced the election would be postponed until 23 October 2011.[[104]](#cite_note-109) International and internal observers declared the vote free and fair. The [Ennahda Movement](/wiki/Ennahda_Movement "Ennahda Movement"), formerly banned under the Ben Ali regime, came out of the election as the largest party, with 89 seats out of a total of 217.[[105]](#cite_note-110) On 12 December 2011, former dissident and veteran human rights activist [Moncef Marzouki](/wiki/Moncef_Marzouki "Moncef Marzouki") was elected president.[[106]](#cite_note-111) In March 2012, Ennahda declared it will not support making sharia the main source of legislation in the new constitution, maintaining the secular nature of the state. Ennahda's stance on the issue was criticized by hardline Islamists, who wanted strict sharia, but was welcomed by secular parties.[[107]](#cite_note-112) On 6 February 2013, [Chokri Belaid](/wiki/Chokri_Belaid "Chokri Belaid"), the leader of the leftist opposition and prominent critic of Ennahda, was assassinated.[[108]](#cite_note-113) In 2014, President [Moncef Marzouki](/wiki/Moncef_Marzouki "Moncef Marzouki") established Tunisia's [Truth and Dignity Commission](/wiki/Truth_and_Dignity_Commission_(Tunisia) "Truth and Dignity Commission (Tunisia)"), as a key part of creating a national reconciliation.[[109]](#cite_note-114)

Tunisia was hit by two terror attacks on foreign tourists in 2015, [first killing 22 people](/wiki/Bardo_National_Museum_attack "Bardo National Museum attack") at the [Bardo National Museum](/wiki/Bardo_National_Museum_(Tunis) "Bardo National Museum (Tunis)"), and [later killing 38 people](/wiki/2015_Sousse_attacks "2015 Sousse attacks") at the [Sousse](/wiki/Sousse "Sousse") beachfront. Tunisian president [Beji Caid Essebsi](/wiki/Beji_Caid_Essebsi "Beji Caid Essebsi") renewed the state of emergency in October for three more months.[[110]](#cite_note-115) The [Tunisian National Dialogue Quartet](/wiki/Tunisian_National_Dialogue_Quartet "Tunisian National Dialogue Quartet") won the 2015 [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") for its work in building a peaceful, pluralistic political order in Tunisia.[[111]](#cite_note-116)

#### Presidency of Kais Saied (2019–present)

Tunisia's first democratically elected president [Beji Caid Essebsi](/wiki/Beji_Caid_Essebsi "Beji Caid Essebsi") died in July 2019.[[112]](#cite_note-117) Following him, [Kais Saied](/wiki/Kais_Saied "Kais Saied") became Tunisia's president after a landslide victory in the [2019 Tunisian presidential elections](/wiki/2019_Tunisian_presidential_election "2019 Tunisian presidential election") in October.[[113]](#cite_note-118) On 23 October 2019, Saied was sworn in as Tunisia's new president.[[114]](#cite_note-119)

On 25 July 2021, amid ongoing demonstrations concerning government dysfunction and corruption and rises in [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") cases, Kais Saied unilaterally [suspended parliament, dismissed the prime minister and withdrew immunity of parliament members](/wiki/2021_Tunisian_political_crisis "2021 Tunisian political crisis").[[115]](#cite_note-120)[[116]](#cite_note-121) In September 2021, Saied said he would appoint a committee to help draft new constitutional amendments.[[117]](#cite_note-122)[[f]](#cite_note-123) On 29 September, he named [Najla Bouden](/wiki/Najla_Bouden "Najla Bouden") as the new prime minister and tasked her with forming a [cabinet](/wiki/Bouden_Cabinet "Bouden Cabinet"), which was sworn in on 11 October.[[118]](#cite_note-124)[[119]](#cite_note-125) On 3 February 2022, Tunisia was voted to the African Union's (AU) Peace and Security Council for the term 2022–2024, according to the Tunisian Foreign Ministry. The poll took place on the fringes of the AU Executive Council's 40th ordinary session, which was held in the Ethiopian capital of Addis Ababa, according to the ministry.[[120]](#cite_note-126)

In February 2022, Tunisia and the International Monetary Fund held preliminary negotiations in the hopes of securing a multibillion-dollar bailout for an economy beset by recession, public debt, inflation, and unemployment.[[121]](#cite_note-127)

In April 2023, the Tunisian government closed the headquarters of the [Ennahda](/wiki/Ennahda "Ennahda") party and arrested its leader [Rached Ghannouchi](/wiki/Rached_Ghannouchi "Rached Ghannouchi").[[122]](#cite_note-128) In October 2023 [Abir Moussi](/wiki/Abir_Moussi "Abir Moussi"), head of the [Free Destourian Party (FDL)](/wiki/Free_Destourian_Party "Free Destourian Party"), became the latest prominent opponent of president Saied to have been detained or imprisoned.[[123]](#cite_note-129)[[124]](#cite_note-130)[[125]](#cite_note-131)[[126]](#cite_note-132) The FDL had emerged from the [Democratic Constitutional Assembly](/wiki/Democratic_Constitutional_Rally "Democratic Constitutional Rally").

In September 2023 Saied had asked to postpone a visit by a delegation of the [EU commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") to discuss migration[[127]](#cite_note-133) according to [Minister of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Interior_(Tunisia) "Ministry of the Interior (Tunisia)") [Kamel Feki](/wiki/Kamel_Feki "Kamel Feki").[[128]](#cite_note-:3-134) Meanwhile, human rights organisations were criticizing the July migration agreement.[[128]](#cite_note-:3-134)[[129]](#cite_note-135)[[130]](#cite_note-136) Feki said that Tunisia, which is one of the most important transit countries for people on their way to Europe,[[131]](#cite_note-:4-137) cannot act as a border guard for other countries.[[132]](#cite_note-138)[[131]](#cite_note-:4-137) In October 2023 Saied turned down 127 million in EU aid[[133]](#cite_note-139) saying that the amount was small and doesn't square with a deal signed three months ago.[[134]](#cite_note-140)[[135]](#cite_note-141) This in turn caused surprise in Brussels.[[136]](#cite_note-142) In April 2025, Tunisia dismantled dozens of makeshift camps housing African migrants, displacing as many as 7,000 people.[[137]](#cite_note-143)[[138]](#cite_note-144)

On 6 October 2024, President Kais Saied won a second term with more than 90% of the vote in a [presidential election](/wiki/2024_Tunisian_presidential_election "2024 Tunisian presidential election") with a 28.8% turnout. Five political parties had urged people to boycott the elections.[[139]](#cite_note-145)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Tunisia](/wiki/Geography_of_Tunisia "Geography of Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Koppen-Geiger_Map_TUN_present.svg/330px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_TUN_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_TUN_present.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") in Tunisia. The climate is Mediterranean towards the coast in the north, while most of the country is desert.

Tunisia is situated on the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") coast of Northwest Africa, midway between the Atlantic Ocean and the [Nile Delta](/wiki/Nile_Delta "Nile Delta"). It is bordered by [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria") on the west (965 km) and southwest and [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya") on the south east (459 km).[[140]](#cite_note-146) It lies between latitudes [30°](/wiki/30th_parallel_north "30th parallel north") and [38°N](/wiki/38th_parallel_north "38th parallel north"), and longitudes [7°](/wiki/7th_meridian_east "7th meridian east") and [12°E](/wiki/12th_meridian_east "12th meridian east"). An abrupt southward turn of the Mediterranean coast in northern Tunisia gives the country two distinctive Mediterranean coasts, west–east in the north, and north–south in the east.

Though it is relatively small in size, Tunisia has great environmental diversity due to its north–south extent. Its east–west extent is limited. Differences in Tunisia, like the rest of the Maghreb, are largely north–south environmental differences defined by sharply decreasing rainfall southward from any point. The Dorsal, the eastern extension of the Atlas Mountains, runs across Tunisia in a northeasterly direction from the Algerian border in the west to the Cape Bon peninsula in the east. North of the Dorsal is the Tell, a region characterized by low, rolling hills and plains, again an extension of mountains to the west in Algeria. In the [Khroumerie](/wiki/Khroumire "Khroumire"), the northwestern corner of the Tunisian Tell, elevations reach 1,050 metres (3,440 ft) and snow occurs in winter.

The [Sahel](/wiki/Sahel,_Tunisia "Sahel, Tunisia"), a broadening coastal plain along Tunisia's eastern Mediterranean coast, is among the world's premier areas of olive cultivation. Inland from the Sahel, between the Dorsal and a range of hills south of Gafsa, are the [Steppes](/wiki/Steppes "Steppes"). Much of the southern region is [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid "Semi-arid") and [desert](/wiki/Desert "Desert").

Tunisia has a coastline 1,148 kilometres (713 mi) long. In maritime terms, the country claims a contiguous zone of 24 nautical miles (44 kilometres), and a territorial sea of 12 nautical miles (22 kilometres).[[141]](#cite_note-147) The city of Tunis is built on a hill slope down to the lake of Tunis. These hills contain places such as Notre-Dame de Tunis, Ras Tabia, La Rabta, La Kasbah, Montfleury and La Manoubia with altitudes just above 50 metres (160 feet). The city is located at the crossroads of a narrow strip of land between Lake Tunis and Séjoumi.[[142]](#cite_note-148)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Water_Stress%2C_Top_Countries_%282020%29.svg/250px-Water_Stress%2C_Top_Countries_%282020%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Water_Stress,_Top_Countries_(2020).svg)

Tunisia is the eighteenth most water stressed country in the world.

[Tunisia's climate](/wiki/Climate_in_Tunisia "Climate in Tunisia") is [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") in the north, with mild rainy winters and hot, dry summers.[[143]](#cite_note-149) The south of the country is desert. The terrain in the north is mountainous, which, moving south, gives way to a hot, dry central [plain](/wiki/Plain "Plain"). The south is [semiarid](/wiki/Semiarid "Semiarid"), and merges into the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara"). A series of [salt lakes](/wiki/Tunisian_salt_lakes "Tunisian salt lakes"), known as *chotts* or *shatts*, lie in an east–west line at the northern edge of the Sahara, extending from the [Gulf of Gabes](/wiki/Gulf_of_Gabes "Gulf of Gabes") into [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria "Algeria"). The lowest point is [Chott el Djerid](/wiki/Chott_el_Djerid "Chott el Djerid") at 17 metres (56 ft) below sea level and the highest is [Jebel ech Chambi](/wiki/Jebel_ech_Chambi "Jebel ech Chambi") at 1,544 metres (5,066 ft).[[144]](#cite_note-150)

Tunisia is home to five terrestrial ecoregions: [Mediterranean conifer and mixed forests](/wiki/Mediterranean_conifer_and_mixed_forests "Mediterranean conifer and mixed forests"), [Saharan halophytics](/wiki/Saharan_halophytics "Saharan halophytics"), [Mediterranean dry woodlands and steppe](/wiki/Mediterranean_dry_woodlands_and_steppe "Mediterranean dry woodlands and steppe"), [Mediterranean woodlands and forests](/wiki/Mediterranean_woodlands_and_forests "Mediterranean woodlands and forests"), and [North Saharan steppe and woodlands](/wiki/North_Saharan_steppe_and_woodlands "North Saharan steppe and woodlands").[[145]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-151)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of Tunisia](/wiki/Politics_of_Tunisia "Politics of Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Kais_Saied_2023.jpg/250px-Kais_Saied_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kais_Saied_2023.jpg)

[Kais Saied](/wiki/Kais_Saied "Kais Saied"), [President](/wiki/President_of_Tunisia "President of Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Sara_Zaafarani_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Sara_Zaafarani_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sara_Zaafarani_(cropped).jpg)

[Sara Zaafarani](/wiki/Sara_Zaafarani "Sara Zaafarani"), [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Tunisia "Prime Minister of Tunisia")

Tunisia is a [presidential republic](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") with a [president](/wiki/President_of_Tunisia "President of Tunisia") serving as [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), a [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Republic_of_Tunisia "Prime Minister of the Republic of Tunisia") as [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Tunisia "Parliament of Tunisia"), and a [civil law court system](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)"). The [Constitution of Tunisia](/wiki/Constitution_of_Tunisia "Constitution of Tunisia"), adopted 26 January 2014, guarantees rights for women and states that the President's religion "shall be Islam". In October 2014 Tunisia held its first elections under the new constitution following the Arab Spring.[[146]](#cite_note-152) Tunisia was the only [democracy in North Africa](/wiki/Democracy_in_Africa "Democracy in Africa") until 2021. After a [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding"), the country now classifies as "hybrid regime" in the [Democracy Index (The Economist)](/wiki/Democracy_Index "Democracy Index").[[147]](#cite_note-153) Between 2020 and 2022 the [V-Dem Democracy indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_indices "V-Dem Democracy indices") score for electoral democracy dropped from 0.727 to 0.307.[[148]](#cite_note-vdem_dataset-154) After the [2022 constitutional referendum](/wiki/2022_Tunisian_constitutional_referendum "2022 Tunisian constitutional referendum"), Tunisia became a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_republic "Presidential republic") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic").

The number of legalized [political parties in Tunisia](/wiki/Political_parties_in_Tunisia "Political parties in Tunisia") has grown considerably since the revolution. There are now over 100 legal parties, including several that existed under the former regime. During the rule of Ben Ali, only three functioned as independent opposition parties: the [PDP](/wiki/Progressive_Democratic_Party_(Tunisia) "Progressive Democratic Party (Tunisia)"), [FDTL](/wiki/Democratic_Forum_for_Labour_and_Liberties "Democratic Forum for Labour and Liberties"), and [Tajdid](/wiki/Ettajdid_Movement "Ettajdid Movement"). While some older parties are well-established and can draw on previous party structures, many of the 100-plus parties extant as of February 2012 are small.[[149]](#cite_note-cnsd-155)

Rare for the Arab world, women held more than 20% of seats in the country's pre-revolution bicameral parliament.[[150]](#cite_note-156) In the 2011 constituent assembly, women held between 24% and 31% of all seats.[[151]](#cite_note-157)[[152]](#cite_note-158) Tunisia is included in the European Union's [European Neighbourhood Policy](/wiki/European_Neighbourhood_Policy "European Neighbourhood Policy") (ENP), which aims at bringing the [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and its neighbours closer. On 23 November 2014, Tunisia held its first presidential election following the Arab Spring in 2011.[[153]](#cite_note-159) The Tunisian legal system is heavily influenced by [French civil law](/wiki/French_civil_law "French civil law"), while the law of personal status is based on Islamic law.[[154]](#cite_note-ri-law-160) [Sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") courts were abolished in 1956.[[154]](#cite_note-ri-law-160)

A [Code of Personal Status](/wiki/Code_of_Personal_Status_(Tunisia) "Code of Personal Status (Tunisia)") was adopted shortly after independence in 1956, which, among other things, gave women full legal status (allowing them to run and own businesses, have bank accounts, and seek passports under their own authority). The code outlawed the practices of polygamy and repudiation and a husband's right to unilaterally divorce his wife.[[155]](#cite_note-161) Further reforms in 1993 included a provision to allow Tunisian women to transmit citizenship even if they are married to a foreigner and living abroad.[[156]](#cite_note-162) The Law of Personal Status is applied to all Tunisians regardless of their religion.[[154]](#cite_note-ri-law-160) The Code of Personal Status remains one of the most progressive civil codes in North Africa and the Muslim world.[[157]](#cite_note-163) On 25 May 2022, President Kais Saied issued a decree for change of constitution by 25 July.[[158]](#cite_note-164) The [referendum](/wiki/2022_Tunisian_constitutional_referendum "2022 Tunisian constitutional referendum") was held that day to a low turnout of 30% of voters, the overwhelming majority of whom accepted the new constitution, strengthening significantly the presidential powers.[[159]](#cite_note-165) Maghreb countries have toughened their tone towards Europe recently.[[160]](#cite_note-166)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Tunisia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Tunisia "Foreign relations of Tunisia")

Tunisia maintains diplomatic relations with over 160+ countries. Former President [Zine El Abidine Ben Ali](/wiki/Zine_El_Abidine_Ben_Ali "Zine El Abidine Ben Ali") has maintained its long-time policy of seeking good relations with the West, while playing an active role in [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") and [African](/wiki/Africa "Africa") regional bodies. President [Habib Bourguiba](/wiki/Habib_Bourguiba "Habib Bourguiba") took a nonaligned stance but emphasized close relations with the European Union, [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[161]](#cite_note-167)

### Military

Main article: [Tunisian Armed Forces](/wiki/Tunisian_Armed_Forces "Tunisian Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/DM-SD-01-05601.jpg/250px-DM-SD-01-05601.jpg)](/wiki/File:DM-SD-01-05601.jpg)

Tunisian military force

As of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tunisia&action=edit), Tunisia had an army of 27,000 personnel equipped with 84 main battle tanks and 48 light tanks. The navy had 4,800 personnel operating 25 patrol boats and 6 other craft. The [Tunisian Air Force](/wiki/Tunisian_Air_Force "Tunisian Air Force") has 154 aircraft and 4 UAVs. Paramilitary forces consisted of a 12,000-member national guard.[[162]](#cite_note-168) Tunisia's military spending was 1.6% of GDP as of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tunisia&action=edit). The army is responsible for national defence and also internal security. Tunisia has participated in peacekeeping efforts in the DROC and Ethiopia/Eritrea.[[163]](#cite_note-169) [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") peacekeeping deployments for the Tunisian armed forces have been in Cambodia ([UNTAC](/wiki/UNTAC "UNTAC")), Namibia ([UNTAG](/wiki/UNTAG "UNTAG")), Somalia, Rwanda, Burundi, Western Sahara ([MINURSO](/wiki/MINURSO "MINURSO")) and the 1960s mission in the Congo, [ONUC](/wiki/ONUC "ONUC").

The military has historically played a professional, apolitical role in defending the country from external threats. Since January 2011 and at the direction of the executive branch, the military has taken on increasing responsibility for domestic security and humanitarian crisis response.[[149]](#cite_note-cnsd-155)

Tunisia is the 73rd most peaceful country in the world, according to the 2024 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index").[[164]](#cite_note-170)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Tunisia](/wiki/Economy_of_Tunisia "Economy of Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/GDP_per_capita_development_of_Tunisia.svg/330px-GDP_per_capita_development_of_Tunisia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_of_Tunisia.svg)

GDP per capita development of Tunisia

Ranked the most competitive economy in Africa by the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") in 2009,[[167]](#cite_note-173) Tunisia is an export-oriented country in the process of liberalizing and privatizing an economy that, while averaging 5% GDP growth since the early 1990s, has suffered from corruption benefiting politically connected elites.[[168]](#cite_note-174) Tunisia's Penal Code criminalises several forms of corruption, including active and passive bribery, abuse of office, extortion and conflicts of interest, but the anti-corruption framework is not effectively enforced.[[169]](#cite_note-175) However, according to the [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index") published annually by [Transparency International](/wiki/Transparency_International "Transparency International"), Tunisia was ranked the least corrupt North African country in 2016, with a score of 41. Tunisia has a diverse economy, ranging from agriculture, mining, manufacturing, and petroleum products, to [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Tunisia "Tourism in Tunisia"), which accounted for 7% of the total GDP and 370,000 jobs in 2009.[[170]](#cite_note-176) In 2008 it had an economy of US$41 billion in nominal terms, and $82 billion in [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity").[[171]](#cite_note-CIA-177)

The agricultural sector accounts for 11.6% of the GDP, industry 25.7%, and services 62.8%. The industrial sector is mainly made up of clothing and footwear manufacturing, production of car parts, and electric machinery. Although Tunisia managed an average 5% growth over the last decade, it continues to suffer from a high unemployment rate, especially among youth.[[172]](#cite_note-178)[[173]](#cite_note-179)[[174]](#cite_note-180)

The European Union remains Tunisia's first trading partner, currently accounting for 72.5% of Tunisian imports and 75% of Tunisian exports. Tunisia is one of the European Union's most established trading partners in the [Mediterranean region](/wiki/Mediterranean_region "Mediterranean region") and ranks as the EU's 30th largest trading partner. Tunisia was the first Mediterranean country to sign an Association Agreement with the European Union, in July 1995, although even before the date of entry came into force, Tunisia started dismantling tariffs on bilateral EU trade. Tunisia finalised the tariffs dismantling for industrial products in 2008 and therefore was the first non-EU Mediterranean country to enter in a free trade area with the EU.[[175]](#cite_note-181)

The consequences of the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine") for the global [food supply](/wiki/Food_supply "Food supply") are being felt particularly strongly in Tunisia.[[176]](#cite_note-182)[[177]](#cite_note-183)

In June 2023 the [World Bank Group](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group") lent Tunisia $268.4 million to finance [ELMED](/wiki/ELMED_interconnector "ELMED interconnector"), an electrical interconnection project with [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") to import electricity generated from [renewable energy sources](/wiki/Energy_transition "Energy transition") to [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") and the EU via a 600 [Megawatt](/wiki/MegaWatt "MegaWatt") [undersea cable](/wiki/Undersea_cable "Undersea cable").[[178]](#cite_note-184)

Tunisia was ranked 76th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[179]](#cite_note-185)[[180]](#cite_note-186)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Tunisia](/wiki/Tourism_in_Tunisia "Tourism in Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Tunezja%2C_Sidi_Bou_Said_-_panoramio_%2810%29.jpg/250px-Tunezja%2C_Sidi_Bou_Said_-_panoramio_%2810%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tunezja,_Sidi_Bou_Said_-_panoramio_(10).jpg)

Sidi Bou Said

Among Tunisia's tourist attractions are its cosmopolitan capital city of [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis"), the ancient ruins of [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage"), the Muslim and Jewish quarters of [Djerba](/wiki/Djerba "Djerba"), coastal resorts outside of [Monastir](/wiki/Monastir,_Tunisia "Monastir, Tunisia"), and the night life-driven city of [Hammamet](/wiki/Hammamet,_Tunisia "Hammamet, Tunisia").[[181]](#cite_note-187) According to *The New York Times*, Tunisia is "known for its golden beaches, sunny weather and affordable luxuries".[[182]](#cite_note-188)[[183]](#cite_note-189)[[184]](#cite_note-190)[[185]](#cite_note-191)[[186]](#cite_note-192)[[187]](#cite_note-193)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Tunisia](/wiki/Energy_in_Tunisia "Energy in Tunisia")

**Sources of electricity production in Tunisia**[[188]](#cite_note-194)

1. Thermal steam (44.0%)
2. Combined cycle (43.0%)
3. Gas turbine (11.0%)
4. Wind, hydroelectric, solar (2.00%)

Tunisia's current power production capacity is estimated at 5,944 [MW](/wiki/Mega_watt "Mega watt") installed in 25 power plants as of 2024. Tunisia produced 19,520 [GWh](/wiki/Gigawatt-hour "Gigawatt-hour") in 2022. State owned power utility company [STEG](/wiki/Tunisian_Company_of_Electricity_and_Gas "Tunisian Company of Electricity and Gas") (Société Tunisienne d'Electricité et de Gas) controls 92.1% of the country's installed power production capacity and produces 83.5% of the electricity.[[189]](#cite_note-195)

Oil production began in 1966 in Tunisia. In 2006 there were 12 oil fields.[[190]](#cite_note-MBendi_economical_information-196) The main field is [El Borma](/wiki/El_Borma "El Borma").[[191]](#cite_note-197) The production rate of crude oil in Tunisia was 29,200 barrels per day (4,640 m3/d) at the end of September 2024.[[192]](#cite_note-198)

Tunisia had [plans for two nuclear power stations](/wiki/Energy_in_Tunisia "Energy in Tunisia"), to be operational by 2020. Both facilities are projected to produce 900–1000 [MW](/wiki/Watt#Megawatt "Watt"). France is set to become an important partner in Tunisia's nuclear power plans, having signed an agreement, along with other partners, to deliver training and technology.[[193]](#cite_note-Reuters-199)[[194]](#cite_note-African_manager-200) As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tunisia&action=edit), Tunisia has abandoned these plans. Instead, Tunisia is considering other options to diversify its energy mix, such as renewable energies, coal, shale gas, liquified natural gas and constructing a submarine power interconnection with Italy.[[195]](#cite_note-201)

According to the Tunisian Solar Plan (which is Tunisia's Renewable Energy Strategy not limited to solar, contrary to what its title may suggest), proposed by the [National Agency for Energy Conservation](http://www.anme.nat.tn/index.php?id=3) ([Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20151021061924/http://www.anme.nat.tn/index.php?id=3) 21 October 2015 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")), Tunisia's objective is to reach a share of 30% of renewable energies in the electricity mix by 2030, most of which should be accounted for by wind power and photovoltaics.[[196]](#cite_note-202) As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tunisia&action=edit), Tunisia had a total renewable capacity of 312 MW (245 MW wind, 62 MW hydropower, 15 MW photovoltaics.)[[197]](#cite_note-203)[[198]](#cite_note-204)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Tunisia](/wiki/Transport_in_Tunisia "Transport in Tunisia")

The country maintains 19,232 kilometres (11,950 mi) of roads,[[171]](#cite_note-CIA-177) with three highways: the [A1](/wiki/A1_motorway_(Tunisia) "A1 motorway (Tunisia)") from Tunis to Sfax (works ongoing for Sfax-Libya), [A3](/wiki/A3_motorway_(Tunisia) "A3 motorway (Tunisia)") Tunis-Beja (works ongoing Beja – Boussalem, studies ongoing Boussalem – Algeria) and [A4](/wiki/A4_motorway_(Tunisia) "A4 motorway (Tunisia)") Tunis – Bizerte. There are 29 airports in Tunisia, with [Tunis Carthage International Airport](/wiki/Tunis_Carthage_International_Airport "Tunis Carthage International Airport") and [Djerba–Zarzis International Airport](/wiki/Djerba%E2%80%93Zarzis_International_Airport "Djerba–Zarzis International Airport") being the most important ones. A new airport, [Enfidha – Hammamet International Airport](/wiki/Enfidha_%E2%80%93_Hammamet_International_Airport "Enfidha – Hammamet International Airport") opened in 2011. The airport is located north of Sousse at Enfidha and is to mainly serve the resorts of Hamammet and Port El Kantaoui, together with inland cities such as Kairouan. Five airlines are headquartered in Tunisia: [Tunisair](/wiki/Tunisair "Tunisair"), [Syphax Airlines](/wiki/Syphax_Airlines "Syphax Airlines"), [Karthago Airlines](/wiki/Karthago_Airlines "Karthago Airlines"), [Nouvelair](/wiki/Nouvelair "Nouvelair"), and [Tunisair Express](/wiki/Tunisair_Express "Tunisair Express"). The railway network is operated by [SNCFT](/wiki/SNCFT "SNCFT") and amounts to 2,135 kilometres (1,327 mi) in total.[[171]](#cite_note-CIA-177) The Tunis area is served by a [Light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") network named *Metro Leger* which is managed by Transtu.

### Water supply and sanitation

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in Tunisia](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Tunisia "Water supply and sanitation in Tunisia")

Tunisia has achieved the highest access rates to water supply and sanitation
services in the Middle East and North Africa. As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tunisia&action=edit), access to
safe drinking water became close to universal approaching 100% in urban
areas and 90% in rural areas.[[199]](#cite_note-JMP-205) Tunisia provides good quality drinking water throughout the year.[[200]](#cite_note-PSP-206)

Responsibility for the water supply systems in urban areas and large rural centers is assigned to the *Sociéte Nationale d'Exploitation et de Distribution des Eaux* (SONEDE), a national water supply authority that is an autonomous public entity under the Ministry of Agriculture. Planning, design, and supervision of small and medium water supplies in the remaining rural areas are the responsibility of the *Direction Générale du Génie Rurale* (DGGR).

In 1974, ONAS was established to manage the sanitation sector. Since 1993, ONAS has had the status of a main operator for the protection of the water environment and combating pollution. The rate of non-revenue water is the lowest in the region at 21% in 2012.[[201]](#cite_note-SONEDE_Chiffres-207)
