### Ethnic groups

According to the [CIA](/wiki/CIA "CIA") [World Factbook](/wiki/The_World_Factbook "The World Factbook"), [ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic_groups "Ethnic groups") in Tunisia are: Arab 98%, European 1%, Jewish and other 1%.[[171]](#cite_note-CIA-177)

According to the 1956 Tunisian census, Tunisia had a population at the time of 3,783,000 residents, 95% consisting of [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") and [Berbers](/wiki/Berbers "Berbers"), 256,000 Europeans and 105,000 Jews. Speakers of [Berber dialects](/wiki/Berber_languages "Berber languages") were 2% of the population.[[203]](#cite_note-209) According to another source, the population of Arabs is estimated to be 98%,[[171]](#cite_note-CIA-177)[[204]](#cite_note-210)[[205]](#cite_note-211) and that of Berbers at 1%.[[206]](#cite_note-Popber1p-212)

Black Tunisians make up 10–15% of the population[[207]](#cite_note-AJrev-213)[[208]](#cite_note-214) and are mostly descended from [sub-Saharan Africans](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") brought to Tunisia as part of the [slave trade](/wiki/Slavery_in_Tunisia "Slavery in Tunisia").[[209]](#cite_note-215)

Amazighs are generally concentrated in the [Dahar mountains](/wiki/Jebel_Dahar "Jebel Dahar") and on the island of [Djerba](/wiki/Djerba "Djerba") in the southeast, and in the [Khroumire](/wiki/Khroumire "Khroumire") mountainous region in the north-west. An important number of genetic and other historical studies point to the predominance of the Amazighs in Tunisia.[[210]](#cite_note-216)

An [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") influence has been particularly significant in forming the [Turco-Tunisian](/wiki/Turks_in_Tunisia "Turks in Tunisia") community. Other peoples have also migrated to Tunisia during different time periods, including West Africans, [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks"), [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals"), [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicians "Phoenicians") ([Punics](/wiki/Punic "Punic")), [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), and French and Italian settlers.[[211]](#cite_note-217) By 1870, the distinction between the Arabic-speaking population and the Turkish elite had blurred.[[212]](#cite_note-218)

From the late 19th century to the period after World War II, Tunisia was home to large populations of [French](/wiki/French_people "French people") and [Italians](/wiki/Italian_Tunisians "Italian Tunisians") (255,000 [Europeans](/wiki/European_Tunisians "European Tunisians") in 1956),[[213]](#cite_note-219) although nearly all of them, along with the Jewish population, left after Tunisia became independent. The [history of the Jews in Tunisia](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Tunisia "History of the Jews in Tunisia") goes back some 2,000 years. In 1948, the Jewish population was estimated at 105,000, but by 2013 only about 1000 remained.[[214]](#cite_note-220)

The first people known to history in what is now Tunisia were the [Berbers](/wiki/Berber_people "Berber people"). Numerous civilizations and peoples have invaded, migrated to, or have been assimilated into the population over the millennia, with influences of population from [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicians "Phoenicians")/[Carthaginians](/wiki/Carthaginians "Carthaginians"), [Romans](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic"), [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals"), [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs"), [Spaniards](/wiki/Spaniard "Spaniard"), [Ottoman Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and [Janissaries](/wiki/Janissaries "Janissaries"), and [French](/wiki/French_people "French people"). There was a continuing inflow of nomadic [Arab tribes](/wiki/Banu_Hilal "Banu Hilal") from the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula").[[56]](#cite_note-stearns-61)

After the [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista") and expulsion of non-Christians and [Moriscos](/wiki/Morisco "Morisco") from Spain, many Spanish [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") and Jews arrived in Tunisia. According to Matthew Carr, "As many as eighty thousand Moriscos settled in Tunisia, most of them in and around the capital, Tunis, which still contains a quarter known as Zuqaq al-Andalus, or Andalusia Alley."[[215]](#cite_note-221)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Tunisia](/wiki/Languages_of_Tunisia "Languages of Tunisia")

[Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") is the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of Tunisia. [Tunisian Arabic](/wiki/Tunisian_Arabic "Tunisian Arabic"), known as Tounsi,[[216]](#cite_note-sayahi-222) is the national, vernacular [variety of Arabic](/wiki/Varieties_of_Arabic "Varieties of Arabic") used by the public.[[217]](#cite_note-223) There is also a small minority of speakers of [Berber languages](/wiki/Berber_language "Berber language") known collectively as Jebbali or Shelha in the country. Actively spoken Berber languages are [Jerba Berber](/wiki/Jerba_Berber "Jerba Berber") on the island of [Djerba](/wiki/Djerba "Djerba") and [Matmata Berber](/wiki/Matmata_Berber "Matmata Berber") in the city of [Matmata](/wiki/Matmata,_Tunisia "Matmata, Tunisia"). The [Sened language](/wiki/Sened_language "Sened language") is extinct.[[218]](#cite_note-224)[[219]](#cite_note-225)

[French](/wiki/French_language "French language") also plays a major role in Tunisian society, despite having no official status. It is widely used in education (e.g., as the language of instruction in the sciences in secondary school), the press, and business. In 2022, there were 6,321,000 French-speakers in Tunisia, or about 52% of the population.[[220]](#cite_note-226) [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") is understood and spoken by a small part of the [Tunisian](/wiki/Tunisian_people "Tunisian people") population.[[221]](#cite_note-227) Shop signs, menus and road signs in Tunisia are generally written in both Arabic and French.[[222]](#cite_note-228)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Tunisia](/wiki/Religion_in_Tunisia "Religion in Tunisia")

Religion in Tunisia ([ARDA](/wiki/Association_of_Religion_Data_Archives "Association of Religion Data Archives") 2020 est.)[[223]](#cite_note-229)

1. [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni "Sunni") (97.5%)
2. [Other Muslim](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (2.00%)
3. [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (0.20%)
4. [None](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") (0.27%)
5. Other (0.03%)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Mosqu%C3%A9e_Zitouna_Kassus-2.jpg/250px-Mosqu%C3%A9e_Zitouna_Kassus-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mosqu%C3%A9e_Zitouna_Kassus-2.jpg)

[Al-Zaytuna Mosque](/wiki/Al-Zaytuna_Mosque "Al-Zaytuna Mosque") in Tunis

Tunisia's constitution declares [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") as the official [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion")—and the absolute majority of its population, or around 98%, report to be [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"), while some 2% follow predominantly [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") or [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism").[[171]](#cite_note-CIA-177) According to a 2018 survey conducted by the [Arab Barometer](/wiki/Arab_Barometer "Arab Barometer"), the vast majority of Tunisians (99.4%) continue to identify as Muslim.[[224]](#cite_note-:0-230) The survey also found that more than one third of Tunisians identify as non-religious. The percentage of Tunisians identifying themselves as non-religious has recently increased from around 12% in 2013 to around 33% in 2018, making Tunisia the least religious country in the Arab world.[[225]](#cite_note-:The_Arab_world_in_seven_charts-231) Nearly half of young Tunisians described themselves as non-religious, according to that same survey.[[226]](#cite_note-arabbarometer.org-232) However, as of July 2022, new surveys by the Arab Barometer say otherwise, particularly BBC's programme, *The Newsroom* journalists highlighting that the previously noted wave of those saying they were not religious has been, in fact, "reversed".[[227]](#cite_note-233) The most recent 2021 Arab Barometer survey reported that 44% of Tunisians consider themselves religious, 37% somewhat religious, and 19% non-religious.[[224]](#cite_note-:0-230)

Tunisians enjoy a significant degree of religious freedom, a right enshrined and protected in its constitution, which guarantees the freedom of thoughts, beliefs and to practice one's religion.[[228]](#cite_note-irfrtun-234) The country has a secular culture where religion is separated from not only political, but also public life. Individual Tunisians are tolerant of religious freedom and generally do not inquire about a person's personal beliefs.[[228]](#cite_note-irfrtun-234)

The bulk of Tunisians belong to the [Maliki school](/wiki/Maliki "Maliki") of [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam"), and their [mosques](/wiki/Mosques "Mosques") are easily recognizable by square minarets. However, the [Turks](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") brought with them the teaching of the [Hanafi school](/wiki/Hanafi "Hanafi") during [Ottoman rule](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), which still survives among [families of Turkish descent](/wiki/Turks_in_Tunisia "Turks in Tunisia") today; their mosques traditionally have octagonal minarets.[[229]](#cite_note-235) Sunnis form the majority, with [non-denominational Muslims](/wiki/Non-denominational_Muslims "Non-denominational Muslims") being the second largest group of Muslims,[[230]](#cite_note-236)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] followed by [Ibadite](/wiki/Ibadite "Ibadite") [Amazighs](/wiki/Amazigh "Amazigh").[[231]](#cite_note-237)[[232]](#cite_note-238)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Catedral_de_Tunis_%282413055515%29.jpg/250px-Catedral_de_Tunis_%282413055515%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catedral_de_Tunis_(2413055515).jpg)

[Cathedral of St. Vincent de Paul](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Vincent_de_Paul "Cathedral of St. Vincent de Paul"), [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis")

The [Church of Carthage](/wiki/Church_of_Carthage "Church of Carthage"), in particular, became significant in the [history of Christianity](/wiki/History_of_Christianity "History of Christianity"), playing a key role in the development of [Christian philosophy](/wiki/Christian_philosophy "Christian philosophy") and [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology"), and producing many prominent religious scholars and theologians.[[233]](#cite_note-239) Prior to independence, Tunisia was home to more than [250,000 Christians](/wiki/European_Tunisians "European Tunisians") (mostly of Italian and Maltese ancestry).[[234]](#cite_note-Decolonization-240) Many Christian Italian settlers left to Italy or France after independence from France.[[235]](#cite_note-241) Today, Tunisia's sizable [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians") community of something over 35,000[[236]](#cite_note-242)[[237]](#cite_note-243) is composed mainly of [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics") (22,000), and to a lesser degree [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"). [Berber Christians](/wiki/Berber_Christians "Berber Christians") continued to live in some [Nefzaoua](/wiki/Nefzaoua "Nefzaoua") villages up until the early fifteenth century,[[238]](#cite_note-244) and the community of [Tunisian Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Africa "Christianity in Africa") existed in the town of [Tozeur](/wiki/Tozeur "Tozeur") up to the 18th century.[[62]](#cite_note-hrbek-67) The International Religious Freedom Report for 2007 estimates that thousands of Tunisian Muslims have [converted to Christianity](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity").[[239]](#cite_note-report-245)[[240]](#cite_note-246)

[Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") is the third largest religion, with between 1,000 and 1,400 members.[[241]](#cite_note-247)[[242]](#cite_note-248) One third of the Jewish population lives in and around the capital. The remainder lives on the island of [Djerba](/wiki/Djerba "Djerba") with 39 synagogues where the Jewish community dates back 2,600 years,[[243]](#cite_note-249) in Sfax, and in Hammam-Lif.[[228]](#cite_note-irfrtun-234) Djerba, an island in the [Gulf of Gabès](/wiki/Gulf_of_Gab%C3%A8s "Gulf of Gabès"), is home to [El Ghriba synagogue](/wiki/El_Ghriba_synagogue "El Ghriba synagogue"), which is one of the [oldest synagogues in the world](/wiki/Oldest_synagogues_in_the_World "Oldest synagogues in the World") and the oldest continuously used. Many Jews consider it a pilgrimage site, with celebrations taking place there once a year due to its age and the legend that the synagogue was built using stones from [Solomon's temple](/wiki/Solomon%27s_temple "Solomon's temple").[[244]](#cite_note-250) Although [antisemitic](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_Tunisia "Antisemitism in Tunisia") violence has been reported,[[245]](#cite_note-251) Tunisia and [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") are said to be the Arab countries most accepting of their Jewish populations.[[246]](#cite_note-252)

There is also a [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") community.[[247]](#cite_note-253)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Tunisia](/wiki/Education_in_Tunisia "Education in Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Coll%C3%A8ge_Sadiki-Kassus.jpg/250px-Coll%C3%A8ge_Sadiki-Kassus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coll%C3%A8ge_Sadiki-Kassus.jpg)

[Sadiki College](/wiki/Sadiki_College "Sadiki College") in [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/UIS_Literacy_Rate_Tunisia_population_plus15_1985_2015.png/250px-UIS_Literacy_Rate_Tunisia_population_plus15_1985_2015.png)](/wiki/File:UIS_Literacy_Rate_Tunisia_population_plus15_1985_2015.png)

Literacy rate of Tunisia population, 15 years and older, 1985–2015. UNESCO Institute of Statistics

The total adult literacy rate in 2008 was 78%[[248]](#cite_note-unescolit-254) and this rate goes up to 97.3% when considering only people from 15 to 24 years old.[[249]](#cite_note-255)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Education is given a high priority and accounts for 6% of [GNP](/wiki/Gross_national_product "Gross national product"). A basic education for children between the ages of 6 and 16 has been compulsory since 1991. Tunisia ranked 17th in the category of "quality of the [higher] educational system" and 21st in the category of "quality of primary education" in [The Global Competitiveness Report](/wiki/The_Global_Competitiveness_Report "The Global Competitiveness Report") 2008–09, released by the World Economic Forum.[[250]](#cite_note-Weforum.org-256)

While children generally acquire [Tunisian Arabic](/wiki/Tunisian_Arabic "Tunisian Arabic") at home, when they enter school at age six, they are taught to read and write in [Standard Arabic](/wiki/Standard_Arabic "Standard Arabic"). From the age of 8, they are taught French while English is introduced at the age of 11.[[251]](#cite_note-257)

The four years of secondary education are open to all holders of Diplôme de Fin d'Études de l'Enseignement de Base where the students focus on entering university level or join the workforce after completion. The Enseignement secondaire is divided into two stages: general academic and specialized. The higher education system in Tunisia has experienced a rapid expansion and the number of students has more than tripled over the past 10 years from approximately 102,000 in 1995 to 365,000 in 2005. The gross enrollment rate at the tertiary level in 2007 was 31 percent, with gender parity index of GER of 1.5.[[250]](#cite_note-Weforum.org-256)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Tunisia](/wiki/Health_in_Tunisia "Health in Tunisia")

In 2010, spending on healthcare accounted for 3.37% of the country's GDP. In 2009, there were 12.02 physicians and 33.12 nurses per 10,000 inhabitants.[[252]](#cite_note-258) The life expectancy at birth was 75.73 years in 2016, or 73.72 years for males and 77.78 years for females.[[253]](#cite_note-259) Infant mortality in 2016 was 11.7 per 1,000.[[254]](#cite_note-260)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Tunisia](/wiki/Culture_of_Tunisia "Culture of Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Le_th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_Tunis_%283301451272%29.jpg/250px-Le_th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_Tunis_%283301451272%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Le_th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_Tunis_(3301451272).jpg)

Municipal Theatre

The culture of Tunisia is mixed due to its long established history of outside influence from people – such as Phoenicians, Romans, Vandals, Byzantines, Arabs, Siculo-Normans, Turks, Italians, Maltese and the French – who all left their mark on the country.[[255]](#cite_note-Rombaldi-261)

### Painting

The birth of Tunisian contemporary painting is strongly linked to the School of Tunis, established by a group of artists from Tunisia united by the desire to incorporate native themes and rejecting the influence of Orientalist colonial painting. It was founded in 1949 and brings together French and Tunisian Muslims, Christians and Jews. Pierre Boucherle was its main instigator, along with [Yahia Turki](/wiki/Yahia_Turki "Yahia Turki"), [Abdelaziz Gorgi](/wiki/Abdelaziz_Gorgi "Abdelaziz Gorgi"), Moses Levy, Ammar Farhat, and Jules Lellouche. Given its doctrine, some members have therefore turned to the sources of aesthetic Arab-Muslim art, such as miniature Islamic architecture, etc. Expressionist paintings by Amara Debbache, Jellal Ben Abdallah, and Ali Ben Salem are recognized while abstract art captures the imagination of painters like Edgar Naccache, Nello Levy, and [Hedi Turki](/wiki/Hedi_Turki "Hedi Turki").[[256]](#cite_note-tangka3-262)

After independence in 1956, the art movement in Tunisia was propelled by the dynamics of nation building and by artists serving the state. A Ministry of Culture was established, under the leadership of ministers such as [Habib Boularès](/wiki/Habib_Boular%C3%A8s "Habib Boularès") who oversaw art and education and power.[[256]](#cite_note-tangka3-262) Artists gained international recognition such as [Hatem El Mekki](/wiki/Hatem_El_Mekki "Hatem El Mekki") or [Zoubeir Turki](/wiki/Zoubeir_Turki "Zoubeir Turki") and influenced a generation of new young painters. Sadok Gmech draws his inspiration from national wealth while Moncef Ben Amor turns to fantasy. In another development, Youssef Rekik reused the technique of painting on glass and founded [Nja Mahdaoui](/wiki/Nja_Mahdaoui "Nja Mahdaoui") calligraphy with its mystical dimension.[[256]](#cite_note-tangka3-262)

There are currently fifty art galleries housing exhibitions of Tunisian and international artists.[[257]](#cite_note-tunisia_online-263) These galleries include Gallery Yahia in Tunis and Carthage Essaadi gallery.[[257]](#cite_note-tunisia_online-263)

A new exposition opened in an old monarchal palace in Bardo dubbed the "awakening of a nation". The exposition boasts documents and artifacts from the Tunisian reformist monarchial rule in mid-19th century.[[258]](#cite_note-264)

### Literature

Main article: [Tunisian literature](/wiki/Tunisian_literature "Tunisian literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Abdelwahab_Meddeb_-_Com%C3%A9die_du_Livre_2011_-_Montpellier_-_P1150907.jpg/250px-Abdelwahab_Meddeb_-_Com%C3%A9die_du_Livre_2011_-_Montpellier_-_P1150907.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abdelwahab_Meddeb_-_Com%C3%A9die_du_Livre_2011_-_Montpellier_-_P1150907.jpg)

[Abdelwahab Meddeb](/wiki/Abdelwahab_Meddeb "Abdelwahab Meddeb"), a Tunisian French-language poet and novelist

Tunisian literature exists in two forms: Arabic and French. Arabic literature dates back to the 7th century with the arrival of Arab civilization in the region. It is more important in both volume and value than French literature, which was introduced during the French protectorate from 1881.[[259]](#cite_note-265)

Among the literary figures include [Ali Douagi](/wiki/Ali_Douagi "Ali Douagi"), who has produced more than 150 radio stories, over 500 poems and folk songs and nearly 15 plays,[[260]](#cite_note-tangka2-266) Khraief Bashir, an Arabic novelist who published many notable books in the 1930s and which caused a scandal because the dialogues were written in Tunisian dialect,[[260]](#cite_note-tangka2-266) and others such as Moncef Ghachem, [Mohamed Salah Ben Mrad](/wiki/Mohamed_Salah_Ben_Mrad "Mohamed Salah Ben Mrad"), or [Mahmoud Messadi](/wiki/Mahmoud_Messadi "Mahmoud Messadi").

As for poetry, Tunisian poetry typically opts for nonconformity and innovation with poets such as [Aboul-Qacem Echebbi](/wiki/Aboul-Qacem_Echebbi "Aboul-Qacem Echebbi").

As for literature in French, it is characterized by its critical approach. Contrary to the pessimism of [Albert Memmi](/wiki/Albert_Memmi "Albert Memmi"), who predicted that Tunisian literature was sentenced to die young,[[261]](#cite_note-267) a high number of Tunisian writers are abroad including [Abdelwahab Meddeb](/wiki/Abdelwahab_Meddeb "Abdelwahab Meddeb"), Bakri Tahar, [Mustapha Tlili](/wiki/Mustapha_Tlili "Mustapha Tlili"), Hele Beji, or Mellah Fawzi. Themes of wandering, exile, heartbreak, disconnection, memory, and representation are often focuses of Tunisian literature.[[262]](#cite_note-268)[[263]](#cite_note-269)

The [national bibliography](/wiki/National_bibliography "National bibliography") lists 1249 non-school books published in 2002 in Tunisia, with 885 titles in Arabic.[[264]](#cite_note-littérature-270) In 2006 this figure had increased to 1,500 and 1,700 in 2007.[[265]](#cite_note-hzreal-271) Nearly a third of the books are published for children.[[266]](#cite_note-272)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Tunisia](/wiki/Music_of_Tunisia "Music of Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Zied_Gharsa_et_la_Rachidia.jpg/250px-Zied_Gharsa_et_la_Rachidia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zied_Gharsa_et_la_Rachidia.jpg)

Rachidia orchestra playing traditional music in Tunis Theater

At the beginning of the 20th century, musical activity was dominated by the liturgical repertoire associated with different religious brotherhoods and secular repertoire which consisted of instrumental pieces and songs in different Andalusian forms and styles of origins, essentially borrowing characteristics of musical language. In 1930 [The Rachidia](/wiki/The_Rachidia "The Rachidia") was founded, well known thanks to artists from the Jewish community. The founding in 1934 of a musical school helped revive Arab Andalusian music largely to a social and cultural revival led by the elite of the time who became aware of the risks of loss of the musical heritage and which they believed threatened the foundations of Tunisian national identity. The institution did not take long to assemble a group of musicians, poets, scholars. The creation of [Radio Tunis](/wiki/Radio_Tunis "Radio Tunis") in 1938 allowed musicians a greater opportunity to disseminate their works, with the station employing a policy of promoting Tunisian musicians exclusively.[[267]](#cite_note-273)

Notable Tunisian musicians include [Saber Rebaï](/wiki/Saber_Reba%C3%AF "Saber Rebaï"), [Dhafer Youssef](/wiki/Dhafer_Youssef "Dhafer Youssef"), [Belgacem Bouguenna](/wiki/Belgacem_Bouguenna "Belgacem Bouguenna"), [Sonia M'barek](/wiki/Sonia_M%27barek "Sonia M'barek"), [Latifa](/wiki/Latifa_(singer) "Latifa (singer)"), [Salah El Mahdi](/wiki/Salah_El_Mahdi "Salah El Mahdi"), [Anouar Brahem](/wiki/Anouar_Brahem "Anouar Brahem"), [Emel Mathlouthi](/wiki/Emel_Mathlouthi "Emel Mathlouthi") and [Lotfi Bouchnak](/wiki/Lotfi_Bouchnak "Lotfi Bouchnak").

### Festivals

Main article: [List of festivals in Tunisia](/wiki/List_of_festivals_in_Tunisia "List of festivals in Tunisia")

Hundreds of international festivals, national, regional or local punctuate the calendar year. Music and theatrical festivals dominate the national cultural scene.

Several festivals take place annually in summer: the [International Festival of Carthage](/wiki/International_Festival_of_Carthage "International Festival of Carthage") in July, the International Festival of Arts of Mahr from late July to early August, and the International Festival of Hammamet in July and August.

The [Carthage Film Festival](/wiki/Carthage_Film_Festival "Carthage Film Festival") is held in October and November of every other year, alternating with the Carthage Theatre Festival. It was created in 1966[[268]](#cite_note-274) by the Tunisian Minister of Culture to showcase films from the [Maghreb](/wiki/Maghreb "Maghreb"), Africa and the Middle East. In order to be eligible for the competition, a film must have a director of African or Middle Eastern nationality, and have been produced at least two years before entry. The grand prize is the *[Tanit d'or](/wiki/Tanit_d%27or "Tanit d'or")*, or "Golden Tanit", named for the [lunar goddess](/wiki/Tanit "Tanit") of ancient [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage"); the award is in the shape of her symbol, a trapezium surmounted by a horizontal line and a circle.

The [International Festival of the Sahara](/wiki/International_Festival_of_the_Sahara "International Festival of the Sahara"), celebrated annually at the end of December, honors the cultural traditions associated with the Tunisian desert. This attracts many tourists and musicians from all around the world, as well as horsemen who flaunt their saddles and local fabrics and skills.

There are also a number of musical festivals; some honor traditional Tunisian music, while others, including the [Tabarka Jazz Festival](/wiki/Tabarka_Jazz_Festival "Tabarka Jazz Festival"), focus on other genres.

In the city of Sousse, the [Carnival of Awussu](/wiki/Carnival_of_Awussu "Carnival of Awussu") is an annual festive and cultural event that unfolds each 24 July. It is a parade of symbolic chariots, [fanfares](/wiki/Fanfare "Fanfare") and folk groups from Tunisia and elsewhere which takes place near the beach of Boujaafar, at the eve of the beginning of '*Awussu* (The word designating the [heat wave](/wiki/Heat_wave "Heat wave") of the month of August according to the [Berber calendar](/wiki/Berber_calendar "Berber calendar")). Originally it was a [Pagan](/wiki/Pagan "Pagan") feast ([Neptunalia](/wiki/Neptunalia "Neptunalia")) celebrating the god of the seas, [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune_(mythology) "Neptune (mythology)") in the [Roman province of Africa](/wiki/Roman_province_of_Africa "Roman province of Africa"), and might even go back to [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") times : the appellation Awussu is a possible deformation of [Oceanus](/wiki/Oceanus "Oceanus").[[269]](#cite_note-275)

[Omek Tannou](/wiki/Omek_Tannou "Omek Tannou") is an ancient Tunisian [rainmaking festival](/wiki/Rainmaking_(ritual) "Rainmaking (ritual)") which was inherited from [Punic](/wiki/Punic "Punic") and [Berber](/wiki/Berbers "Berbers") traditions involving invocations of the goddess [Tanit](/wiki/Tanit "Tanit"). It features the ritual use of the [sculpted](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture") head of a woman (somewhat resembling the head of a girl's [doll](/wiki/Doll "Doll")), which is carried in procession between the houses of a village during periods of [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought") by children singing the refrain أمك طانقو يا نساء طلبت ربي عالشتاء (transliteration: *amk ṭangu ya nsaʾ tlbt rbi ʿalshta'a*), "Amek tango, o women, ask God to rain". This song varies according to the region because the term *shta* designates rain only in certain urban areas. Each housewife then pours a little water on the statuette, invoking rain.

### Media

Main article: [Media of Tunisia](/wiki/Media_of_Tunisia "Media of Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/BatimentTelevisionTunisienneAvril2012.jpg/250px-BatimentTelevisionTunisienneAvril2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:BatimentTelevisionTunisienneAvril2012.jpg)

Headquarters of [Télévision Tunisienne](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9vision_Tunisienne "Télévision Tunisienne") since March 2010

The TV media has long remained under the domination of the [Establishment of the Broadcasting Authority Tunisia](/wiki/ERTT "ERTT") (ERTT) and its predecessor, the Tunisian Radio and Television, founded in 1957. On 7 November 2006, President Zine el-Abidine Ben Ali announced the demerger of the business into two separate companies, which became effective on 31 August 2007. Until then, ERTT managed all public television stations ([Télévision Tunisienne 1](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9vision_Tunisienne_1 "Télévision Tunisienne 1") as well as Télévision Tunisienne 2 which had replaced the defunct RTT 2) and four national radio stations ([Radio Tunis](/wiki/Radio_Tunis "Radio Tunis"), Tunisia Radio Culture, Youth and [RTCI](/wiki/Radio_Tunis_Cha%C3%AEne_Internationale "Radio Tunis Chaîne Internationale")) and five regional stations in Sfax, Monastir, Gafsa, Le Kef and Tataouine. Most programs are in Arabic, but some are in French. Growth in private sector radio and television broadcasting has seen the creation of numerous operations including Radio Mosaique FM, Jawhara FM, Zaytuna FM, [Hannibal TV](/wiki/Hannibal_TV "Hannibal TV"), Ettounsiya TV, and [Nessma TV](/wiki/Nessma_TV "Nessma TV").[[270]](#cite_note-276)[[271]](#cite_note-277)

In 2007, some 245 newspapers and magazines (compared to only 91 in 1987) are 90% owned by private groups and independents.[[272]](#cite_note-278) The Tunisian political parties have the right to publish their own newspapers, but those of the opposition parties have very limited editions (like Al Mawkif or Mouwatinoun). Before the recent democratic transition, although freedom of the press was formally guaranteed by the constitution, almost all newspapers have in practice followed the government line report. Critical approach to the activities of the president, government and the Constitutional Democratic Rally Party (then in power) were suppressed. In essence, the media was dominated by state authorities through the [Agence Tunis Afrique Presse](/wiki/Agence_Tunis_Afrique_Presse "Agence Tunis Afrique Presse"). This has changed since, as the media censorship by the authorities have been largely abolished, and self-censorship has significantly decreased.[[273]](#cite_note-freedomhouse.org-279) Nonetheless, the current regulatory framework and social and political culture mean that the future of press and media freedom is still unclear.[[273]](#cite_note-freedomhouse.org-279) In September 2022 Tunisian president Kais Saied signed [Decree Law 54](/wiki/Decree_Law_54_(Tunisia) "Decree Law 54 (Tunisia)"), which purported to combat "[false information and rumours](/wiki/Fake_news "Fake news")" on the Internet. Article 24 of the decree gives up to five years imprisonment and a fine of up to 50,000 [dinar](/wiki/Tunisian_dinar "Tunisian dinar") for anyone found to be spreading such information. This is doubled if the offending statement is made about a state official.[[274]](#cite_note-280)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Tunisia](/wiki/Sport_in_Tunisia "Sport in Tunisia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/CA_-_Rad%C3%A8s.jpg/250px-CA_-_Rad%C3%A8s.jpg)](/wiki/File:CA_-_Rad%C3%A8s.jpg)

[Stade Hammadi Agrebi](/wiki/Stade_Olympique_Hammadi_Agrebi "Stade Olympique Hammadi Agrebi") in [Radès](/wiki/Rad%C3%A8s "Radès")

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the most popular sport in Tunisia. The [Tunisia national football team](/wiki/Tunisia_national_football_team "Tunisia national football team"), also known as "The Eagles of Carthage", won the [2004 African Cup of Nations](/wiki/2004_African_Cup_of_Nations "2004 African Cup of Nations") ([ACN](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations")) as hosts.[[275]](#cite_note-281)[[276]](#cite_note-282) They also represented Africa in the [2005 FIFA Cup of Confederations](/wiki/2005_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2005 FIFA Confederations Cup"), which was held in Germany, but they could not go beyond the first round.

Their premier football league is the [Tunisian Ligue Professionnelle 1](/wiki/Tunisian_Ligue_Professionnelle_1 "Tunisian Ligue Professionnelle 1"). The main clubs are [Espérance Sportive de Tunis](/wiki/Esp%C3%A9rance_Sportive_de_Tunis "Espérance Sportive de Tunis"), [Étoile Sportive du Sahel](/wiki/%C3%89toile_Sportive_du_Sahel "Étoile Sportive du Sahel"), [Club Africain](/wiki/Club_Africain "Club Africain"), [Club Sportif Sfaxien](/wiki/Club_Sportif_Sfaxien "Club Sportif Sfaxien"), [Union Sportive Monastirienne](/wiki/US_Monastir_(football) "US Monastir (football)"), [Stade Tunisien](/wiki/Stade_Tunisien "Stade Tunisien"), and [CA Bizertin](/wiki/CA_Bizertin "CA Bizertin").

The [Tunisia men's national handball team](/wiki/Tunisia_men%27s_national_handball_team "Tunisia men's national handball team") has participated in several handball world championships. In 2005, Tunisia came fourth. The [national league](/wiki/Tunisian_Handball_League "Tunisian Handball League") consists of about 12 teams, with [Club Africain](/wiki/Club_Africain_(handball) "Club Africain (handball)") and [Espérance](/wiki/Esp%C3%A9rance_Sportive_de_Tunis_(handball) "Espérance Sportive de Tunis (handball)") dominating. The most famous Tunisian handball player is [Wissem Hmam](/wiki/Wissem_Hmam "Wissem Hmam"). In the [2005 Handball Championship in Tunis](/wiki/2005_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "2005 World Men's Handball Championship"), Wissem Hmam was ranked as the top scorer of the tournament. The Tunisian national handball team won the African Cup ten times, being the team dominating this competition. The Tunisians won the 2018 African Cup in Gabon by defeating Egypt.[[277]](#cite_note-283)

[Tunisia's national basketball team](/wiki/Tunisia%27s_national_basketball_team "Tunisia's national basketball team") has emerged as a top side in Africa. The team won the [2011 Afrobasket](/wiki/2011_Afrobasket "2011 Afrobasket") and hosted Africa's top basketball event in 1965, 1987 and 2015. Tunisia was one of the continent's pioneers in basketball as it established one of Africa's first competitive leagues.[[278]](#cite_note-284)[[279]](#cite_note-285)[[280]](#cite_note-286)

In [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing"), [Victor "Young" Perez](/wiki/Victor_Perez_(Tunisian_boxer) "Victor Perez (Tunisian boxer)") was world champion in the flyweight weight class in 1931 and 1932.[[281]](#cite_note-287)

In the [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"), Tunisian [Oussama Mellouli](/wiki/Oussama_Mellouli "Oussama Mellouli") won a gold medal in 1500 meter freestyle.[[282]](#cite_note-288) In the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), he won a bronze medal in the 1500 meter freestyle and a gold medal in the [men's marathon swim](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_marathon_10_kilometre "Swimming at the 2012 Summer Olympics – Men's marathon 10 kilometre") at a distance of 10 kilometers.

In 2012, Tunisia participated for the seventh time in its history in the [Summer Paralympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Paralympic_Games "Summer Paralympic Games"). Their national team finished the competition with 19 medals; 9 golds, 5 silvers and 5 bronzes. Tunisia was classified 14th on the [Paralympics medal table](/wiki/2012_Summer_Paralympics_medal_table "2012 Summer Paralympics medal table") and 5th in [Athletics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Paralympics_medal_table "2012 Summer Paralympics medal table").

Throughout the years 2021 to 2023, tennis saw a spike of popularity in Tunisia and other Arabic countries as tennis player [Ons Jabeur](/wiki/Ons_Jabeur "Ons Jabeur") rapidly moved up the rankings reaching a career high ranking of number 3, and making 3 grand slam finals, including 2 at [Wimbledon](/wiki/2023_Wimbledon_Championships "2023 Wimbledon Championships").

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** English is taught from fourth grade,[[3]](#cite_note-3) and there is a notable increase in its usage among the youth and a growing demand in the workforce.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** Pronunciation: [UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/tjuːˈnɪziə, -ˈnɪs-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/-ˈniːʒə, -ˈniːʃə, -ˈnɪʒə, -ˈnɪʃə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[18]](#cite_note-19) [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): تونس *[Tūnis](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/8/80/Tounes_bel_tounsi.wav/Tounes_bel_tounsi.wav.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Tounes_bel_tounsi.wav "File:Tounes bel tounsi.wav")*, pronounced [[tuː.nɪs]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Arabic "Help:IPA/Arabic")
3. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الجمهورية التونسية *[al-Jumhūrīya at-Tūnisīya](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/4/45/Tounesjmr.wav/Tounesjmr.wav.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Tounesjmr.wav "File:Tounesjmr.wav")*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *République tunisienne*. The native Arabic official name translates more closely to "Tunisian Republic", as does the commonly used French translation, but the English translation "Republic of Tunisia" is used in English even by the Tunisian government for official purposes (e.g., the designation used by the Tunisian embassy in Washington, D.C.)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") and [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") are [confessional democracies](/wiki/Confessionalism_(politics) "Confessionalism (politics)").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-43)** The *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")* reads "TUNIS, Regency of, formerly one of the Barbary states of north Africa, but since 1881 a dependency of France,..." in its 1875–1889 ninth edition,[[34]](#cite_note-38) but a 1902–1903 supplement (the tenth edition) and its celebrated eleventh edition (which reads "TUNISIA (Regency of Tunis), a country of North Africa, under the protection of France,..."[[35]](#cite_note-39)).
   The *[New International Encyclopedia](/wiki/New_International_Encyclopedia "New International Encyclopedia")* 1905 and 1916 editions read "TUNIS (Fr. *Tunisie*). A French protectorate in North Africa.",[[36]](#cite_note-40)[[37]](#cite_note-41) while the 1928 supplement reads "TUNIS or Tunisia. A French protectorate in North Africa..."[[38]](#cite_note-42)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-123)** The new constitution, which increased presidential powers, was passed in a [referendum](/wiki/2022_Tunisian_constitutional_referendum "2022 Tunisian constitutional referendum") the following year with a turnout of only around 30%, amidst a widespread boycott.
